summary: Gruber marvels at Mark Gurman's extraordinarily comprehensive pre-WWDC 2024 leak report at Bloomberg, which reads as though sourced from someone who already watched the keynote. He emphasizes that the leak is not deliberate and that Apple insiders are furious about it. Gruber then digs into the specifics, raising pointed questions about Apple's rumored OpenAI-powered chatbot — where it would live, how on-device processing would work across devices, and what distinguishes it from OpenAI's own ChatGPT apps. He also comments on AI-generated emoji, Messages improvements, and corrects Gurman's framing of Safari ad-blocking as a privacy headache, arguing it would actually be an antitrust headache.
key_points: Gurman's leak is so comprehensive it appears sourced from someone who watched the WWDC keynote in advance, and Apple insiders are reportedly furious about it

The rumored Apple Intelligence chatbot powered by OpenAI raises unresolved UX questions: where it lives, whether conversations sync across devices, and how on-device processing handles device-specific data
Gruber questions the value proposition of an Apple-branded OpenAI chatbot when ChatGPT already has excellent native iOS and Mac apps
Built-in Safari ad blocking would not be a privacy headache but an antitrust one, since Apple benefits from steering advertisers toward its own App Store ads
Apple's ATT framework already lets Apple leverage its own first-party data (which apps users own) for ad targeting while restricting third-party tracking
money_quotes: His report reads as though he's gotten the notes from someone who's already watched Monday's keynote.
The thing I'm most insatiably curious about is how he got all this. Who spilled? By what means? It's extraordinary.
Don't think for a second it's a deliberate leak. Folks inside Apple are, I assure you, furious about this, and incredulous that one of their own colleagues would leak it to Gurman.
If he switched to a different beat we'd be almost entirely in the dark; as it stands, he's seemingly spoiled most of what's coming Monday.
On-device processing raises questions that don't exist with cloud-only processing.
Their new Mac app is quite sweet — written in AppKit and SwiftUI, not some turd of a web wrapper like most such AI chatbots.
Built-in ad blocking in Safari wouldn't be a privacy headache; blocking ads can only increase privacy. It would be an antitrust/regulatory headache.
key_insight: Gurman's unprecedented pre-WWDC leak dump reveals nearly the entire keynote, but the most interesting unanswered questions are about the practical UX and architectural challenges of Apple's on-device AI strategy.
